author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
What is the Meaning of ‘In Whom All Have Sinned’?
title: What is the Meaning of ‘In Whom All Have Sinned’?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But these speak thus who wish to wrest men from the apostle’s words into their own thought.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For where the apostle says, “By one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin, and so passed upon all men,” they will have it there understood not that “sin” passed over, but “death.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: What, then, is the meaning of what follows, “Whereto all have sinned”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For either the apostle says that in that “one man” all have sinned of whom he had said, “By one man sin entered into the world,” or else in that “sin,” or certainly in “death.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For it need not disturb us that he said not “in which” [using the feminine form of the pronoun], but “in whom” [using the masculine] all have sinned; since “death” in the Greek language is of the masculine gender.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Let them, then, choose which they will,—for either in that “man” all have sinned, and it is so said because when he sinned all were in him; or in that “sin” all have sinned, because that was the doing of all in general which all those who were born would have to derive; or it remains for them to say that in that “death” all sinned.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But in what way this can be understood, I do not clearly see.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For all die in the sin; they do not sin in the death; for when sin precedes, death follows—not when death precedes, sin follows.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Because sin is the sting of death—that is, the sting by whose stroke death occurs, not the sting with which death strikes.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Just as poison, if it is drunk, is called the cup of death, because by that cup death is caused, not because the cup is caused by the death, or is given by death.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But if “sin” cannot be understood by those words of the apostle as being that “wherein all have sinned,” because in Greek, from which the Epistle is translated, “sin” is expressed in the feminine gender, it remains that all men are understood to have sinned in that first “man,” because all men were in him when he sinned; and from him sin is derived by birth, and is not remitted save by being born again.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For thus also the sainted Hilary understood what is written, “wherein all have sinned;” for he says, “wherein,” that is, in Adam, “all have sinned.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Then he adds, “It is manifest that all have sinned in Adam, as it were in the mass; for he himself was corrupted by sin, and all whom he begot were born under sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: When he wrote this, Hilary, without any ambiguity, indicated how we should understand the words, “wherein all have sinned.”
